title: A guide to the 'Library of Congress Control Number' (LCCN)

interlinking_suggestions.title: How to Write a Book to Establish Your Freelance Expertise
How to get an 'ISBN' for your self-published book

author.name: Sofia Gonzalez
author.bio: A successful freelance creative director, Sofia provides insights for designers, writers, and artists. She covers topics like pricing creative work, protecting intellectual property, and building a powerful personal brand.

key_takeaways: Secure a single LCCN to establish institutional credibility and library access for your core intellectual work, while using separate ISBNs for commercial sales tracking of each format (hardcover, e-book).
Prioritize obtaining an LCCN if your non-fiction book is intended to establish thought leadership, is aimed at academic or research audiences, or is meant to build a long-term author brand.
Apply for your free LCCN well before publication, as you must print the assigned number on your book's copyright page and send one physical copy to the Library of Congress to fulfill the requirement.
Understand that an LCCN is for print editions only; use a unique ISBN as the essential identifier for the sales, tracking, and distribution of your digital formats like e-books and audiobooks.
summary: For author-entrepreneurs, the Library of Congress Control Number (LCCN) is often a misunderstood administrative task. This article advises that securing a free LCCN for your print book is a strategic move to establish institutional credibility, distinguishing it from the commercially-focused ISBN. By doing so, you transform your manuscript into a durable professional asset, signaling legitimacy and making your work discoverable and acquirable by libraries and academic institutions.
ai_overview_answer: A Library of Congress Control Number (LCCN) is a free identifier that establishes a permanent bibliographic record for a book in the Library of Congress, signaling professional credibility to libraries and academic institutions. Unlike an ISBN, which is used for commercial sales of specific formats like paperbacks or e-books, a single LCCN identifies the core intellectual work across all its print editions. Securing an LCCN is a strategic asset for non-fiction books, academic works, or local histories, as it significantly increases the likelihood of library acquisition. This is because the number makes a book easily discoverable and verifiable for librarians, who prioritize works already indexed in national databases. To obtain one, publishers must apply through the free Preassigned Control Number (PCN) program before publication using a purchased ISBN. The Library of Congress does not assign LCCNs to digital-only formats like e-books, audiobooks, journals, or workbooks. After receiving the number and printing it on the copyright page, you are required to send one physical copy of the book to the Library of Congress to fulfill the agreement.

itemListElement.name: A guide to the 'Library of Congress Control Number' (LCCN)

@graph.headline: A guide to the 'Library of Congress Control Number' (LCCN)
@graph.description: For author-entrepreneurs, the Library of Congress Control Number (LCCN) is often a misunderstood administrative task. This article advises that securing a free LCCN for your print book is a strategic move to establish institutional credibility, distinguishing it from the commercially-focused ISBN. By doing so, you transform your manuscript into a durable professional asset, signaling legitimacy and making your work discoverable and acquirable by libraries and academic institutions.

step.name: The Pre-Application Checklist
step.text:
Anxiety often stems from the unknown. Eliminate it by preparing everything you need before you open the application portal. Having these details finalized turns a daunting task into a simple ten-minute exercise.

*   Your Final Book Title: It must be the settled-upon title.
*   Publisher & Author Name: As a self-publishing professional, this is likely you or your registered business.
*   Your ISBN: You must have a purchased ISBN before applying. Free ISBNs provided by some platforms are not eligible.
*   Projected Publication Date: An educated estimate is sufficient.
*   Approximate Page Count: Your best projection for the final length of the print book.

step.name: The PCN Application: A Step-by-Step Guide
step.text: The Library of Congress manages this process through its Preassigned Control Number (PCN) program, accessed online via the PrePub Book Link portal. Self-publishers can create an account and begin the request immediately. The portal will guide you through entering your prepared metadata. Pay close attention to the "U.S. City of Publication," which must be a city in the United States listed on your title or copyright page. Common pitfalls, like entering a draft title or incorrect ISBN, are the primary reasons for rejection. By using your checklist, you ensure your application is approved the first time.

step.name: Post-Submission Workflow
step.text:
After you submit your request, the process is straightforward:

1.  Approval: Expect to receive your PCN—which becomes your LCCN upon publication—via email within one to two weeks.
2.  Copyright Page Placement: This is the final, crucial step. Once you receive the number, you must add it to your book's copyright page in the correct format: Library of Congress Control Number: 20259XXXXX
3.  Fulfilling Your Obligation: The PCN program requires you to send one physical copy of your "best edition" (e.g., the hardcover) to the Library of Congress immediately upon publication. This completes the agreement and makes your book eligible for selection into the Library's collections.

mainEntity.name: How much does an LCCN cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: The LCCN is free. The Library of Congress provides the number through its PCN program without charge. Your only direct cost is for the one physical copy of your book (and postage) that you are obligated to send to the Library upon publication.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between an LCCN and an ISBN?

acceptedAnswer.text: The LCCN is for library access and cataloging, identifying the core intellectual work for institutional tracking. The ISBN is for commercial sales and distribution, identifying specific formats of that work (e.g., hardcover, e-book) for sale.

mainEntity.name: Can a self-published author get an LCCN?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. The Library of Congress provides a portal specifically for authors and self-publishers in the U.S. to apply for an LCCN, ensuring that professionally produced independent works can be properly cataloged.

mainEntity.name: Do I need an LCCN for my e-book?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. The Library of Congress does not assign an LCCN to books published only in digital formats. For e-books, the ISBN is the essential identifier for all commercial and distribution purposes.

mainEntity.name: How long does it take to get an LCCN?

acceptedAnswer.text: The process typically takes one to two weeks. It is wise to apply well in advance of your publication date to account for periods of high volume.

mainEntity.name: Does an LCCN guarantee my book will be in the Library of Congress?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. An LCCN is assigned to your book's bibliographic record, making it possible for the Library to track and catalog your work. It does not guarantee your book will be selected for the permanent collection, which is a separate curatorial decision.

body.content: For the leader of a Business-of-One, every decision must be strategic. This is especially true when publishing a book, where the alphabet soup of identifiers can feel like a distraction. But one of these—the Library of Congress Control Number (LCCN)—is not an administrative task. It is a strategic lever for elevating your intellectual property, transforming a manuscript into a durable professional asset.
This is not about compliance; it's about commanding respect for your work in the circles that matter most.
Beyond a Number: Your IP's Permanent Address

First, let's demystify the LCCN. Think of it not as a random string of digits, but as a permanent, unique address for your book's catalog record within the Library of Congress—the world's largest library. Strictly speaking, the number applies to the bibliographic record of your work, not the physical book itself. This establishes a formal, verifiable record that librarians and acquisition professionals use to locate your book in national databases. For you, the author-entrepreneur, it is the first step in creating a piece of professional legacy.
LCCN vs. ISBN: The Professional's Decision Framework

This is where many professionals get stuck, but the distinction is clear and strategic. An International Standard Book Number (ISBN) is essential for commerce; an LCCN is vital for credibility and institutional access. An ISBN is assigned to each specific format of your book—hardcover, paperback, and audiobook will all have different ISBNs—and is used by retailers to track sales. The LCCN, however, identifies the core intellectual work itself, meaning one number covers all print editions.
Here is a simple framework to guide your thinking:

rows: Enables your book to be sold and tracked in the global marketplace.
Signals professionalism and makes it easy for institutions to acquire and catalog your work.

body.content: The Tangible Return on Institutional Credibility

For a Business-of-One, every action must have a tangible return. While the LCCN itself is free, the credibility it confers delivers real value. Securing an LCCN signals to the market that you are a serious publisher who understands industry standards. This enhances your book's authority and significantly increases the likelihood of it being considered for library purchasing orders. Librarians with limited resources prioritize acquiring books that are already indexed in their systems; the LCCN is the key that unlocks that door.
A Simple Decision Checklist: Do You Need an LCCN?
To eliminate any remaining uncertainty, use this direct checklist. If you answer "yes" to any of the following, securing an LCCN is a strategic move.

items: Are you publishing a non-fiction book to establish expertise or thought leadership?
Is your book intended for use in academic, research, or professional training settings?
Are you publishing a work of biography, genealogy, or local history?
Is your target audience likely to seek out your book through library systems?
Do you want to build a long-term author brand on par with traditional houses?

body.content: If your project is a journal, a workbook, or a digital-only e-book, an LCCN is not applicable, as the Library of Congress does not assign them to these formats. This clarity allows you to focus your energy where it matters most.
Securing Your LCCN: A Professional Workflow

With a clear "yes" in hand, execution becomes the focus. Transforming that strategic decision into a professional asset is a matter of a simple, controlled process. This is not a bureaucratic maze; it is a straightforward workflow you can complete with confidence.
The Pre-Application Checklist

Anxiety often stems from the unknown. Eliminate it by preparing everything you need *before* you open the application portal. Having these details finalized turns a daunting task into a simple ten-minute exercise.

items: **Your Final Book Title:** It must be the settled-upon title.
**Publisher & Author Name:** As a self-publishing professional, this is likely you or your registered business.
**Your ISBN:** You must have a purchased ISBN before applying. Free ISBNs provided by some platforms are not eligible.
**Projected Publication Date:** An educated estimate is sufficient.
**Approximate Page Count:** Your best projection for the final length of the print book.

body.content: The PCN Application: A Step-by-Step Guide

The Library of Congress manages this process through its Preassigned Control Number (PCN) program, accessed online via the PrePub Book Link portal. Self-publishers can create an account and begin the request immediately. The portal will guide you through entering your prepared metadata. Pay close attention to the "U.S. City of Publication," which must be a city in the United States listed on your title or copyright page. Common pitfalls, like entering a draft title or incorrect ISBN, are the primary reasons for rejection. By using your checklist, you ensure your application is approved the first time.
Post-Submission Workflow
After you submit your request, the process is straightforward:

items: **Approval:** Expect to receive your PCN—which becomes your LCCN upon publication—via email within one to two weeks.

**Copyright Page Placement:** This is the final, crucial step. Once you receive the number, you must add it to your book's copyright page in the correct format:
    **Library of Congress Control Number: 20259XXXXX**

**Fulfilling Your Obligation:** The PCN program requires you to send one physical copy of your "best edition" (e.g., the hardcover) to the Library of Congress immediately upon publication. This completes the agreement and makes your book eligible for selection into the Library's collections.

body.content: Integrating Your LCCN into a Modern Publishing Strategy

The metadata you create in the LCCN process is the invisible engine of discoverability. That engine must power more than a single printed volume; it must drive the discoverability of your entire intellectual property ecosystem. In a world of multi-format releases, your authority depends on a clear, unified strategy for identifying each component of your work.
Navigating Digital Formats

Let’s be direct: The Library of Congress does not assign an LCCN to books published only in digital formats, such as e-books or audiobooks. This fact often creates a sense of anxiety—a fear that a digital product is somehow less legitimate.

This is the wrong frame. The solution is not to find a workaround for the LCCN but to use the correct identifier for the correct channel. For all digital channels, your **ISBN is the single most critical identifier for sales, tracking, and discovery**. The LCCN anchors your work to the library world; the ISBN anchors it to the commercial one.
The Hybrid Identifier Framework

To maintain control, think of your work as a core asset with multiple expressions. The LCCN acts as the permanent, foundational identifier for the core work, anchored to its first print expression. From that anchor, you assign a unique ISBN to each format you release. This creates a clear, professional hierarchy that prevents confusion for distributors, retailers, and libraries.
Here is what that system looks like in practice:

rows: Library cataloging, permanent archival record, institutional discovery.
Commerce and supply chain tracking for this specific physical product.
Commerce and supply chain tracking for this specific physical product.
Digital commerce, sales tracking, and distribution to online retailers.
Sales and distribution tracking on platforms like Audible and Libby.

body.content: This framework puts you in complete command of your product ecosystem, ensuring every format is correctly cataloged and commercially trackable.
Print-on-Demand (POD) and the LCCN

Many professionals leverage print-on-demand (POD) services. A common misconception is that these books are not eligible for an LCCN. This is incorrect. POD *print* books are absolutely eligible, provided they meet the standard requirements.
The process integrates seamlessly into your pre-publication workflow:

items: Apply for and receive your PCN *before* you finalize your interior files.
Place the number on your copyright page.
Upload your final, compliant files to your chosen POD service.
Upon publication, send one physical copy of the finished POD book to the Library of Congress to fulfill your obligation.

body.content: This ensures your work, even without a traditional print run, is properly registered and available for acquisition by libraries, maximizing your reach and credibility.
Frequently Asked Questions
Executing the small details correctly builds a durable legacy. Here are direct answers to the most common queries about the Library of Congress Control Number.

items:
**How much does an LCCN cost?**
The LCCN is free. The Library of Congress provides the number through its PCN program without charge. Your only direct cost is for the one physical copy of your book (and postage) that you are obligated to send to the Library upon publication.

**What is the difference between an LCCN and an ISBN?**
The LCCN is for library access and cataloging, identifying the core intellectual work for institutional tracking. The ISBN is for commercial sales and distribution, identifying specific formats of that work (e.g., hardcover, e-book) for sale.

**Can a self-published author get an LCCN?**
Yes. The Library of Congress provides a portal specifically for authors and self-publishers in the U.S. to apply for an LCCN, ensuring that professionally produced independent works can be properly cataloged.

**Do I need an LCCN for my e-book?**
No. The Library of Congress does not assign an LCCN to books published only in digital formats. For e-books, the ISBN is the essential identifier for all commercial and distribution purposes.

**How long does it take to get an LCCN?**
The process typically takes one to two weeks. It is wise to apply well in advance of your publication date to account for periods of high volume.

**Does an LCCN guarantee my book will be in the Library of Congress?**
No. An LCCN is assigned to your book's *bibliographic record*, making it possible for the Library to track and catalog your work. It does not guarantee your book will be selected for the permanent collection, which is a separate curatorial decision.

body.content: Your LCCN: The Final Checkpoint in Professional Publishing

Obtaining a Library of Congress Control Number is more than an administrative task; it is a declaration of intent. It is the final, definitive signal that you, the CEO of your Business-of-One, have not just created a manuscript but have produced a professional-grade intellectual asset ready for the world's most respected institutions.

Think of it as the final quality-assurance checkpoint. Securing an LCCN formalizes your effort, creating a bibliographic record that allows your work to be found and cataloged by librarians and acquisition systems. It is the official entry of your asset into the national database, a move that fundamentally enhances its discoverability and credibility.

By following this strategic playbook, you have moved from uncertainty to confident execution. You now understand that this number is not a bureaucratic hurdle but a key that unlocks a new level of market access and institutional legitimacy. You have moved beyond simply writing a book to *professionally publishing* one, equipping your work with the official identifier it needs to endure.
